items.q: What is the difference between nuclear fission and fusion?
items.correct: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into smaller ones; fusion joins light nuclei into a heavier one.
items.misconception: Fission and fusion are the same process, just at different scales.
items.random_wrong: Fission combines small atoms into bigger ones, and fusion splits them.
items.why: The words sound similar (both start 'fi-'), and students mix up which one combines and which one splits.

items.q: If air resistance is ignored, which falls faster: a heavy ball or a light ball dropped from the same height?
items.correct: Both fall at the same rate because gravitational acceleration is independent of mass.
items.misconception: Heavier objects fall faster than lighter objects because gravity pulls more on them.
items.random_wrong: The lighter ball falls faster because it has less inertia.
items.why: Students reason 'more gravity force = more acceleration' but forget the heavier object also has proportionally more inertia, cancelling out.

items.q: What is the threshold frequency in the photoelectric effect?
items.correct: The minimum frequency of light required to eject electrons from a given metal — it equals the work function divided by Planck's constant.
items.misconception: The minimum intensity of light needed to eject electrons.
items.random_wrong: The frequency at which photons travel at the speed of light.
items.why: Students substitute 'intensity' for 'frequency' or describe threshold as a brightness limit, missing the quantum (per-photon) condition.
The answer examiners want is: hf. “hλ” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: de Broglie wavelength of e⁻ moving at v:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: h/p. “h/m” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Metal with Φ = 2 eV. Light of f corresponding to E = 3 eV. KE_max in eV:
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
